title: Senate Targets India With 100% Russia Oil Tariffs as Bank of Korea Starts Tightening, Warsh Draws the Line
subhead: A bipartisan Senate bill proposes 100% tariffs on countries buying Russian oil, with India the most exposed target, as the Bank of Korea broke a three-and-a-half-year freeze with its first rate hike. Fed Chair Warsh used his first congressional testimony to set a hawkish tone despite June's best inflation data in years, while the US-Iran ceasefire collapsed into full military escalation.

sections.heading: Senate Sanctions Bill Proposes 100% Tariffs on Russian Oil Buyers, Targets India
sections.body:
A bipartisan group of US senators unveiled the Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026, which would impose tariffs of up to 100% on countries that continue importing Russian crude oil. The legislation specifically names India, China, Slovakia, Hungary, and Azerbaijan as the five target nations.

The revised bill reduced the maximum penalty from an earlier draft's 500% to 100%, but the scope remains sweeping. It exempts approximately 15 European countries and Japan that import less than 15% of Russia's natural gas exports or are taking steps to reduce dependence on Moscow.

Indian officials reacted with sharp criticism, accusing Washington of 'double standards' for shielding European purchasers while penalizing New Delhi. A senior Indian official told The Economic Times: 'Making India a punching bag for US foreign policy failures will be counterproductive.' India imports over 88% of its crude oil requirements from overseas markets and has increased Russian crude purchases since 2022 to manage domestic inflation and energy security.

The bill, initially championed by the late Senator Lindsey Graham and backed by President Trump, faces procedural hurdles. Democratic lawmakers raised concerns that it grants Trump broad new trade powers over countries like India and Japan. The legislation marks the first time Congress has explicitly authorized broad tariffs against third-party countries for supporting a foreign government's wartime economy.
sections.whyItMatters: This bill would weaponize tariffs as a foreign policy tool in an unprecedented way — penalizing not Russia directly, but its customers. If enacted, it would force a choice between Russian crude and US market access for India and China, the world's two largest incremental oil buyers. The exemption for European nations creates a credibility problem that New Delhi is already exploiting diplomatically, and the bill's tariff powers could further fracture global trade architecture.

sections.heading: Bank of Korea Hikes Rates for First Time in 3.5 Years, Signals More Tightening
sections.body:
South Korea's central bank raised its benchmark rate by 25 basis points to 2.75% on Thursday, ending a three-and-a-half-year easing pause. The Bank of Korea's board voted to tighten as inflation remains well above its 2% target at 3.2%, driven by a semiconductor-led economic boom and currency pressures.

Governor Rhee Chang-yong signaled further hikes ahead, with Reuters reporting that economists forecast the rate reaching 3.0% by year-end. The move marks the start of what investors expect will be a sustained tightening cycle extending into 2027.

The BOK has increasingly argued that the current chip upcycle differs from previous ones because it is being driven by structural AI-related demand rather than cyclical inventory rebuilding. The won had been under pressure from Middle East energy uncertainty, adding to the case for tightening.

Meanwhile, the Bank of Canada held its overnight rate at 2.25% on Wednesday for a sixth consecutive meeting, noting that growth would strengthen in the second half of the year while inflation pressures remained contained.
sections.whyItMatters: The BOK's move makes it the latest major Asian central bank to shift toward tightening as the Middle East conflict stokes inflationary pressures through energy channels. The juxtaposition with the Bank of Canada's hold illustrates a growing global monetary policy divergence: commodity-importing Asian economies face stronger inflation impulses than some Western peers, forcing earlier action even as growth risks from the trade war mount.

sections.heading: Fed Chair Warsh Vows No Tolerance for Inflation in First Congressional Testimony
sections.body:
New Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh delivered his first congressional testimony on July 14-15, telling the House Financial Services Committee that the central bank has 'no tolerance for persistently elevated inflation.' He also flagged AI investment as a new inflation driver and suggested a potential review of the Fed's toolkit for addressing price pressures.

The testimony came as June inflation data showed significant cooling. Consumer prices fell 0.4% month-on-month in June, the largest monthly drop in four years, bringing the annual rate down to 3.5% from 4.2% in May. Core CPI remained stable at 2.6% annually. Producer prices also unexpectedly fell 0.3% in June, the biggest decline in 14 months, driven by a 12% plunge in gasoline prices.

Despite the cooler data, Warsh emphasized that inflation remains well above the Fed's 2% target. Traders now expect the Fed to skip a rate hike at the July meeting but anticipate a potential increase by September, given persistent inflationary risks from the Iran conflict and AI-driven investment demand.

Warsh also faced questions about his regular meetings with the Trump administration, pledging to 'do my job' if challenged politically — his most direct comment yet on Fed independence, according to NPR and Reuters.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh is setting a hawkish tone despite the best inflation print in years. The contradiction is deliberate: the Iran conflict has injected energy price uncertainty that could reverse June's soft numbers quickly. His mention of AI as an inflation driver is a new framing that could justify tighter policy even if traditional CPI measures cool further.

sections.heading: US Restores Iran Naval Blockade, Strikes Expand Northward
sections.body:
The United States reimposed a full naval blockade on Iranian ports on Wednesday, ordering warships and aircraft to stop vessels entering or leaving Iran. CENTCOM confirmed it launched two waves of strikes on Iranian military targets — the second reaching further north than previous rounds — aimed at degrading capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

US forces also disabled a Curaçao-flagged oil tanker, the Belma, that was attempting to reach Kharg Island in violation of the blockade. CENTCOM said the vessel ignored multiple warnings. The incident marks the first known US seizure of a commercial ship under the blockade order.

Iran's Revolutionary Guard responded by threatening to close additional oil export routes, declaring 'the region's oil and gas exports will either be available to everyone or to no one.' Iran then launched retaliatory missile strikes targeting US military bases in Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan, according to Iranian state media and confirmed by US Central Command, which accused Iran of attacking seven commercial ships and launching missiles against Gulf states.

Kpler, the maritime tracking firm, recorded just 21 ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, with 56 confirmed incidents and 17 seafarer fatalities reported in the waterway since hostilities began. Analysts at Goldman Sachs warned Brent crude could exceed $110 per barrel if the conflict continues to widen.
sections.whyItMatters: The ceasefire that briefly calmed markets in June is now formally dead. The US has moved from escort operations to an active maritime blockade with enforcement by force, while Iran has expanded the conflict zone beyond Hormuz by threatening Bab el-Mandeb and striking Gulf state host nations. The risk of a prolonged closure of two of the world's most vital energy chokepoints is now the baseline scenario.

sections.heading: Oil Rises for Fourth Day as Conflict Escalation Deepens Supply Fears
sections.body:
Brent crude futures climbed 0.4% to $85.28 a barrel on Thursday, while WTI rose 0.5% to $80.02, extending a four-day rally. Both benchmarks are hovering near one-month highs touched earlier in the week.

Oil prices have been climbing since the US-Iran ceasefire unravelled last week. The renewed naval blockade and Iran's threats to expand disruptions to Bab el-Mandeb — the gateway to the Suez Canal — have added a second potential chokepoint to the existing Hormuz disruption.

India raised its windfall tax on diesel and jet fuel exports by Rs 7 per litre on Tuesday, while cutting the levy on petrol, as domestic supply concerns mount alongside global price pressures. European aviation fuel supply has tightened to roughly 80% of normal levels due to the conflict's impact on Middle East imports.

Nissan Securities chief strategist Hiroyuki Kikukawa noted that while markets see full-scale war as unlikely, 'WTI could still rise to $85–$87 depending on how the conflict develops.'
sections.whyItMatters: The oil market is pricing in a scenario where neither the Hormuz ceasefire nor the blockade is temporary. India's windfall tax increase signals that major importers are already rationing supply through fiscal tools. European jet fuel stress suggests the supply chain disruption is spreading beyond crude into refined products.

sections.heading: Senate Democrats Block $1.15 Trillion Defense Bill Over Iran War
sections.body:
Senate Democrats voted as a unified bloc to block the $1.15 trillion National Defense Authorization Act on Tuesday, falling short of the 60 votes needed to advance the legislation. The procedural vote failed 50-46 along party lines.

Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer said: 'Donald Trump does not get to drag the American people deeper into a war he cannot explain and does not know how to end — and then demand that Congress look the other way.' Democrats argued that passing the NDAA would amount to endorsing Trump's handling of the Iran war.

The vote came the same day the White House formally notified Congress that it had resumed bombing strikes against Iran, effectively ending the fragile ceasefire.

House Republicans immediately countered by unveiling a $95 billion supplemental spending package for the Iran war, farm aid, and elections, planning to use the filibuster-proof budget reconciliation process to bypass Democratic votes entirely.

Congress has voted more than 10 times on war powers resolutions to halt hostilities, but none has succeeded. The NDAA has passed every year for over 60 years, making this blockade a rare procedural break with tradition.
sections.whyItMatters: The NDAA blockade is a significant escalation in Congressional opposition to the Iran war. While Democrats lack the votes to stop military action outright, they can deny procedural cover and funding mechanisms. The Republican pivot to budget reconciliation for war spending would set a precedent — using a process designed for deficit reduction to fund an overseas conflict with no sunset clause.

sections.heading: US PPI Posts Largest Drop in 14 Months, But Energy Outlook Darkens
sections.body:
The US Producer Price Index fell 0.3% from May to June, the largest monthly decline since April 2025 and a sharp reversal from the 0.6% increase the month before. Compared to a year earlier, wholesale prices were up 5.5%, decelerating from 6% in May.

The drop was driven almost entirely by energy: gasoline prices plunged 12% in June, though they remain nearly 43% higher than a year earlier due to the Iran war's impact on global supply. Core wholesale prices, excluding food and energy, rose 0.2% from May and 4.7% annually.

TradeStation's global head of market strategy David Russell cautioned: 'Energy saved the day in June, but that might become ancient history if the Strait of Hormuz doesn't open soon.' The PPI data captures the period before the US re-imposed its naval blockade on Iran on July 14-15, meaning the July numbers will likely tell a very different story.
sections.whyItMatters: June's PPI and CPI data look like good news in the rearview mirror, but they measure an economy before the ceasefire collapsed. The 12% gasoline drop that drove the improvement has already been reversed by this week's blockade-driven price surge. The Fed's dilemma — cool data but hot forward expectations — is about to get sharper.

bottomLine: The Sanctioning Russia Act would, if enacted, open a second trade-war front — this one targeting India and China over Russian oil purchases, with 100% tariffs as the enforcement mechanism. It joins a worsening picture: the US-Iran ceasefire is functionally over, the Bank of Korea has started tightening, and Fed Chair Warsh is holding a hawkish line even as June's inflation data came in soft. The good inflation numbers are almost certainly a lagging indicator — they reflect a month when energy prices fell on ceasefire hopes that have since collapsed. July's data will capture the blockade's price shock, and the Senate sanctions bill, if it advances, would compound it by disrupting Russian crude flows to the world's fastest-growing oil market.
sources: Reuters, NPR, AP News, Independent, Al Jazeera, News18, Crypto Briefing, SFGate/AP, Financial Express, Firstpost, Straits Times, Yahoo Finance, Business Standard, AOL/Reuters, Nissan Securities, TradeStation, Kpler, Goldman Sachs
